name: Qalupalik
description: Scaly, humanoid creatures that dwell beneath the sea ice. They are notorious for kidnapping children who wander too close to the shoreline, stuffing them into a large amauti (pouch) to take them to their underwater homes.
appearance: Qalupalik takes the form of a aquatic-humanoid known in Canada folklore.
behavior: Known for drowning ability, aquatic speed, water manipulation.
cultural_significance: Inuit cautionary tale to keep children away from dangerous water. Explains child drownings.
summary: A scaly sea-creature that kidnaps children who wander too close to the ice
fun_fact: The Qalupalik is a scaly aquatic creature of Inuit lore that kidnaps disobedient children from the shoreline.
seo_description: The Qalupalik is a scaly aquatic creature of Inuit lore that kidnaps disobedient children from the shoreline.
source: Inuit spiritual tradition; documented in Arctic ethnographic records.

name: Qalupalik
description: Qalupalik is a humanoid from Greenland mythology. They are notorious for kidnapping children who wander too close to the shoreline, stuffing them into a large amauti (pouch) to take them to their underwater homes.
appearance: Qalupalik takes the form of a aquatic-humanoid known in Greenland folklore.
behavior: Qalupalik is dangerous, prey, teach, wander in Greenland folklore.
habitat: Cold Arctic waters, beneath ice floes, along shorelines near settlements. Lurks just below the surface, hidden from view. Most active during summer when children play near the water and during spring thaw when ice edges are unstable.
cultural_significance: Qalupalik is the most active cautionary figure in modern Greenland, still used by parents today to keep children safe from the very real danger of drowning in cold Arctic waters. It is the Greenlandic equivalent of the bogeyman, specifically adapted to the marine environment.
summary: A scaly sea-creature that kidnaps children who wander too close to the ice
fun_fact: The Qalupalik is a scaly aquatic creature of Inuit lore that kidnaps disobedient children from the shoreline.
seo_description: The Qalupalik is a scaly aquatic creature of Inuit lore that kidnaps disobedient children from the shoreline.
source: Inuit folklore; sea-creatures known as Qalupalik who kidnap children, common in Greenlandic oral traditions.

name: Qarakorshaq
description: Qarakorshaq is a monster from Turkmenistan folklore. It is said to dwell in Dark paths, shadows. In folklore, it is described as hides in shadows, devours travelers. dangerous night creature. Turkmen night predator. Represents dangers of traveling at night.
appearance: Animal-like, shadowy, predator. Hides in darkness.
behavior: Hides in shadows, devours travelers. Dangerous night creature.
cultural_significance: Turkmen night predator. Represents dangers of traveling at night.
summary: Animal-like creature that hides in shadows and devours unwary travelers
fun_fact: Animal-like creature that hides in shadows and devours unwary travelers.
seo_description: Animal-like creature that hides in shadows and devours unwary travelers.

name: Qat
description: Qat is a deity from Vanuatu folklore. It is said to dwell in Banks Islands. In folklore, it is described as created islands, humanity, night. established customs. Vanuatu creator god. Important in Banks Islands.
appearance: Creator deity, main god of region.
behavior: Created islands, humanity, night. Established customs.
cultural_significance: Vanuatu creator god. Important in Banks Islands.
summary: Main god of Banks Islands who created islands, humanity, night, and established social customs
fun_fact: Main god of Banks Islands who created islands, humanity, night, and established social customs.
seo_description: Main god of Banks Islands who created islands, humanity, night, and established social customs.
source: Vanuatu mythology — Qat is the creator deity of the Banks Islands, documented in Codrington's "The Melanesians".

name: Qilin
description: A benevolent hooved chimerical creature that appears in times of peace or to herald the birth of a great leader. 'Giraffe' in modern Chinese.
appearance: Dragon-like head with antlers, scaled body, hooves, and flowing mane. Often depicted with flames around the feet.
behavior: Gentle, peaceful, never harms insects or grass. Walks on clouds. Peaceful omen that brings good fortune.
cultural_significance: One of the four most sacred Chinese animals. Associated with wisdom, prosperity, and benevolent rule.
summary: A benevolent hooved chimerical creature that appears in times of peace or to herald the birth of a great leader
fun_fact: A benevolent hooved chimerical creature that appears in times of peace or to herald the birth of a great leader. 'Giraffe' in modern Chinese.
seo_description: A benevolent hooved chimerical creature that appears in times of peace or to herald the birth of a great leader. 'Giraffe' in modern Chinese.
source: Chinese/East Asian mythology; the Qilin is a benevolent chimerical creature.

name: Quetzalcoatl
description: THE FEATHERED SERPENT — the most important syncretic deity in Mexican and Mexican-American spiritual folklore. In Aztec tradition, Quetzalcóatl was the god of wind (Ehecatl), knowledge, learning, the priesthood, and the morning star (Venus). He is credited with creating humanity by descending to Mictlan (the underworld) and mixing his own blood with the bones of previous generations to create a new race of people. He is the inventor of books, the calendar, and the arts of civilization. The feathered serpent iconography appears throughout Mesoamerica from Teotihuacan (200 BCE) to the Aztec Empire, with his worship centered at Cholula's great pyramid — the largest pyramid by volume in the world. Quetzalcóatl's name combines 'quetzal' (the brilliant green bird) and 'coatl' (serpent), symbolizing the union of earth and sky. In one of his most famous myths, he was tricked by Tezcatlipoca into getting drunk and sleeping with his sister, leading to his exile — but he promised to return from the east. This prophesied return was later fatally conflated with Hernán Cortés's arrival in 1519, a complex historical narrative that continues to shape Mexican-American understanding of conquest and cultural survival. In Chicano spirituality, Quetzalcóatl has been reclaimed as a powerful symbol of indigenous identity, cultural resistance, and the profound wisdom of pre-Columbian civilizations.
appearance: A massive serpent covered in the brilliant emerald-green feathers of the quetzal bird, often depicted with a plumed headdress, a white star (Venus) on his chest, and sometimes with human features. In Aztec codices, he is shown as both a fully serpentine figure and as a human wearing a conical cap and conch-shell pectoral. His serpent body is adorned with symbols of wind and movement.
behavior: Brings civilization, knowledge, and the arts to humanity. Governs the wind, the morning star, and the forces of creation. As a creator deity, he descended to the underworld Mictlan to gather the bones of past generations and create new humans. He taught humanity the calendar, writing, and agriculture. Associated with learning, the priesthood, and cultural advancement. His prophesied return was misinterpreted by the Aztecs as Cortés's arrival, leading to the fall of the empire.
habitat: Temples and sacred sites across Mesoamerica, the sky (as the morning star Venus), the underworld Mictlan
cultural_significance: The supreme Aztec creator god of wind, knowledge, and civilization, whose worship dates back to Teotihuacan (200 BCE). His pyramid at Cholula is the largest by volume in the world. The legend of his prophesied return was tragically conflated with Hernán Cortés's arrival, a narrative that shaped the conquest of Mexico. In Chicano spirituality, Quetzalcóatl has been reclaimed as the ultimate symbol of indigenous identity, cultural resistance, and the enduring wisdom of pre-Columbian civilizations. Represents the profound synthesis of earth and sky, matter and spirit, that characterizes Mesoamerican cosmology.
summary: THE FEATHERED SERPENT — supreme Aztec creator god of wind and knowledge, whose prophesied return was conflated with Cortés, now a Chicano symbol of indigenous resistance
fun_fact: The Great Pyramid of Cholula, dedicated to Quetzalcóatl, is the largest pyramid by volume on Earth — 4.45 million cubic meters, surpassing even the Great Pyramid of Giza
seo_description: Feathered serpent god of wind, learning, and civilization
source: Mesoamerican mythology, Popol Vuh & Florentine Codex

name: Quirquincho Vivo
description: Quirquincho Vivo is a cryptid from Argentina folklore. It is said to dwell in Argentine territory. In folklore, it is described as brings good fortune. killing brings curse. Argentine sacred cryptid. Fortune bringer. Associated with Argentine wilderness, Argentine forests, Argentine lakes. Possesses adaptation, stealth, stone manipulation.
appearance: Quirquincho Vivo appears as a cryptid in Argentina mythology.
summary: Quirquincho Vivo is a cryptid from the folklore and mythology of Argentina.
fun_fact: Living sacred armadillo bringing good fortune. Killing one brings terrible curse.
seo_description: Living sacred armadillo bringing good fortune. Killing one brings terrible curse.
source: Argentine Chilean folklore — Quirquincho Vivo armadillo cryptid.

source_references.b: The Muisca: Lords of the Andes

name: Qutb Shah
description: Pakistani ghost of a Sufi saint who died unjustly. Protects the oppressed, helps those in need. Haunts shrines and nearby areas.
appearance: Old man with long beard, wearing simple clothes, halo of light. Kind eyes, peaceful smile.
behavior: Blesses the honest, punishes the cruel. Appears in dreams, gives guidance. Can cause illness to wrongdoers.
habitat: Sufi shrines, old mosques, places of injustice
cultural_significance: Sufi tradition of saintly intervention after death. Wali (saint) spirits.
summary: Qutb Shah is a ghost from the folklore and mythology of Pakistan.
fun_fact: Pakistani ghost of a Sufi saint who died unjustly. Protects the oppressed, helps those in need. Haunts shrines and nearby areas.
seo_description: Pakistani ghost of a Sufi saint who died unjustly. Protects the oppressed, helps those in need. Haunts shrines and nearby areas.
source: Pakistan mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Quỷ
description: Vietnamese demon or evil spirit. Can be fallen spirit, cursed person, or black magic creature. Causes harm, possesses people, eats humans.
appearance: Red or black skin, sharp teeth and claws, horns. Glowing eyes. Demonic features. Can be invisible.
behavior: Hunts at night, possesses vulnerable people. Can be exorcised by monks. Feeds on fear and flesh. Afraid of temples and amulets.
cultural_significance: General evil spirit concept. Mix of Buddhist and folk beliefs.
summary: Quỷ is a demon from the folklore and mythology of Vietnam.
fun_fact: Vietnamese demon or evil spirit. Can be fallen spirit, cursed person, or black magic creature. Causes harm, possesses people, eats humans.
seo_description: Vietnamese demon or evil spirit. Can be fallen spirit, cursed person, or black magic creature. Causes harm, possesses people, eats humans.
source: Vietnamese folk religion — Quỷ demon figure.

name: Qutrub
description: The Qutrub (also spelled Qutrub) is a type of werehyena from Arabian folklore, a shape-shifting creature that combines human and hyena traits. According to the 10th-century scholar al-Mas'udi, the Qutrub was created from a split egg of a female jinn — its counterpart being the Marid (powerful sea jinn). The Qutrub is described as resembling a cat-like creature that haunts graveyards, eating corpses. Pre-Islamic Arabs considered the Qutrub a type of jinn or demon closely associated with death and transformation. Like the European werewolf but with a hyena as its base form, the Qutrub can shift between human and animal form at night. The hyena's scavenging habits, unsettling calls that resemble human laughter, and presence near burial grounds made it a natural candidate for a creature associated with death and deception. The Qutrub is less known than the Ghul but represents an important strand of Arabian shapeshifter mythology tied to specific animals.
appearance: Hyena-like creature with supernatural qualities. In human form, retains some hyena features — sloping back, sharp teeth, unsettling laugh. Cat-like in some descriptions.
behavior: Haunts graveyards and eats corpses. Nocturnal shape-shifter. Can appear as a human during the day and revert to hyena form at night.
habitat: Graveyards, cemeteries, desert edges, abandoned places of the Arabian Peninsula
cultural_significance: Represents Arabian lycanthropy traditions distinct from European werewolf myths. The hyena's real-world behaviors — scavenging graves, laughing calls, and nocturnal habits — shaped this mythological figure. Demonstrates how environment shapes supernatural beliefs.
summary: Werehyena from Arabian folklore, a shapeshifting jinn that haunts graveyards and feeds on corpses, combining human and hyena traits
fun_fact: The word Qutrub comes from the same Arabic root as 'qatar' (drip), possibly referring to the creature's drooling or the dripping of blood from its mouth after feeding.
seo_title: Qutrub – The Corpse-Eating Werehyena of Arabian Folklore
seo_description: The Qutrub is a shapeshifting werehyena from Arabian folklore that haunts graveyards and feeds on corpses, a unique Arabian lycanthrope tradition.
source: Al-Mas'udi, Muruj al-Dhahab (Meadows of Gold); al-Jahiz; Fee, Mythology in the Middle Ages

source_references.t: Qutrub: Wolf-demon of Saudi Arabian Folklore

name: Qalupalik
description: Qalupalik is a humanoid from Inuit mythology. They are notorious for kidnapping children who wander too close to the shoreline, stuffing them into a large amauti (pouch) to take them to their underwater homes.
appearance: Qalupalik takes the form of a aquatic-humanoid known in Greenland folklore.
behavior: Qalupalik is kidnapping, lurking, predatory in Greenland folklore.
habitat: Beneath the sea ice and along the frozen shores
cultural_significance: A cautionary figure used by parents to keep children safe from the extremely dangerous Arctic shoreline.
summary: A scaly sea-creature that kidnaps children who wander too close to the ice
fun_fact: The Qalupalik is a scaly aquatic creature of Inuit lore that kidnaps disobedient children from the shoreline.

name: Qwaxw (Raven)
description: Qwaxw is the Nuxalk name for Raven, the culture hero who brought light to the world. Created by Yulm from charcoal, Qwaxw was given wings, eyes, and life by the Four Carpenters. He flew to Nusmata to retrieve the sun from Alhkw'ntam box. He transformed into mud in Skimina drinking water, was swallowed, and was born as her child. As Raven-child he cried for the sun box until Alhkw'ntam relented. Qwaxw broke the globe, and light leaked through the smoke vent.
appearance: Qwaxw is a raven created from charcoal, a glossy black bird that can transform into human form or into small objects like mud.
behavior: Qwaxw is a trickster who brings light through persistent scheming. He transforms himself to achieve his goals and uses deception.
habitat: The Nuxalk world, particularly the upper world (Nusmata) and the human world.
cultural_significance: Qwaxw is the culture hero of Nuxalk mythology, responsible for bringing light.
summary: Qwaxw (Raven) is the Nuxalk culture hero created by the Four Carpenters who brought light to the world.
fun_fact: Qwaxw (Raven) is the Nuxalk culture hero created by the Four Carpenters who brought light to the world.
source: Franz Boas, The Mythology of the Bella Coola Indians (1898)

name: Qamata
description: Qamata (also known as uThixo) is the supreme creator god of the Xhosa people who existed before all things. From a primordial void of darkness and silence, Qamata created the world using Uhlanga (sacred reeds) as the material for all life. He divided existence into three realms: the sky for himself, the earth for humans, and the underworld for ancestors. He defeated the chaos giant Iqungqungqu to bring order to the cosmos, but remains distant from daily affairs, working through ancestors and natural signs with thunder and the sun representing his power.
appearance: Invisible, omnipresent being. Manifested through natural phenomena: thunder, lightning, sun rays.
behavior: Distant creator who works through intermediaries. Punishes wrongdoing through natural disasters. Rewards virtue through abundance.
cultural_impact: The name uThixo was adopted by Xhosa Christians for the Christian God, making Qamata central to the syncretic religious identity of the Xhosa people today.
symbolism: Three realms (sky, earth, underworld); sun as divine eye; sacred reeds as origin of life
fun_fact: The Xhosa name for God, 'uThixo,' may derive from the Khoisan word for 'spirit' and was adopted by early Christian missionaries to refer to the Christian deity, creating a direct bridge between traditional and Christian faiths.
summary: Xhosa supreme creator god who formed the world from sacred reeds and defeated the chaos giant
source: W.D. Hammond-Tooke, The Bantu-Speaking Peoples of Southern Africa (1974)
cultural_significance: Qamata is a a deity from Southern Africa mythology, representing important cultural values and traditional beliefs.

source_references.t: Qamata: Supreme Being of the Xhosa

name: Qals Mink Coast Salish
description: Qals Mink is a trickster hero from Coast Salish folklore who adventures through transformation, often imitating greater beings with mixed results.
appearance: Qals Mink Coast Salish is a animal-spirit from Canada tradition.
behavior: The Qals Mink Coast Salish is a animal-spirit from Indigenous North American folklore with documented traditional stories.

summary: Qals Mink is a trickster hero from Coast Salish folklore who adventures through transformation, often imitating greater beings with mixed results.
fun_fact: A animal-spirit figure from Indigenous North American mythology.
source: Coast Salish spiritual tradition; documented in Northwest Coast ethnographic records.

name: Qwaxw Raven Nuxalk
description: Qwaxw is the Nuxalk name for Raven, the trickster who brought daylight, fire, and salmon to the Nuxalk people through clever theft.
appearance: Qwaxw Raven Nuxalk is a animal-spirit from Canada tradition.
behavior: The Qwaxw Raven Nuxalk is a animal-spirit from Indigenous North American folklore with documented traditional stories.
cultural_significance: Qwaxw is the Nuxalk name for Raven, the trickster who brought daylight, fire, and salmon to the Nuxalk people through clever theft
summary: Qwaxw is the Nuxalk name for Raven, the trickster who brought daylight, fire, and salmon to the Nuxalk people through clever theft.
